source_title: Do you think the lack of affordable homes is a significant problem in the United States? / Do you support or oppose proposed federal legislative reforms to improve housing affordability? [Multiple hou
question: Do you think the lack of affordable homes is a significant problem in the United States? / Do you support or oppose proposed federal legislative reforms to improve housing affordability? [Multiple housing policy items tested]
method: Online survey, weighted by age, gender, race, educational attainment, region
editor_notes: 74% say lack of affordable homes is a significant problem (83% Democrats, 71% independents, 68% Republicans). Bipartisan majority supports federal action on housing. No single binary support/oppose question; this is the headline finding. Commissioned by BPC/NHC.

source_title: How concerned are you about the cost of housing? [Very concerned / Somewhat concerned / Not too concerned / Not at all concerned]
question: How concerned are you about the cost of housing? [Very concerned / Somewhat concerned / Not too concerned / Not at all concerned]
method: Pew Research Center survey (mode not specified in this article)
editor_notes: 69% of Americans say they are 'very concerned' about the cost of housing, up from 61% in April 2023. Exact sample size and fielding date not published in this short-read; analysis references a 2024 Pew survey. Pew's primary data source is U.S. Census Bureau ACS for housing statistics.

source_title: Do you support or oppose the following statement: 'The government should increase spending on affordable housing because it has a responsibility to ensure taxpayers' essential needs can be met' vs. 'T
question: Do you support or oppose the following statement: 'The government should increase spending on affordable housing because it has a responsibility to ensure taxpayers' essential needs can be met' vs. 'The government should not increase spending on affordable housing because it would cause budget strain and national debt concerns'?
method: Web panel, weighted by age, gender, education, race, geography, recalled presidential vote
editor_notes: 61% agree government should increase affordable housing spending; 28% oppose. Survey also found 72% support low-interest loans for affordable housing construction, 72% support requiring developers to limit rent/price hikes after green building improvements. Commissioned by Data for Progress on green housing.

source_title: Do you support or oppose action to prevent anticompetitive behavior by large corporate landlords using algorithmic tools to share market data, limit competition, and raise rents? [Multiple housing pol
question: Do you support or oppose action to prevent anticompetitive behavior by large corporate landlords using algorithmic tools to share market data, limit competition, and raise rents? [Multiple housing policy items including rent caps tested]
method: Web panel, weighted by age, gender, education, race, geography, 2020 recalled vote
editor_notes: 78% support action to prevent algorithmic rent price-fixing (54% strongly support — highest of any policy tested). 77% agree 'America has a housing shortage.' 64% agree 'The government should have a role in the housing market.' Commissioned by Center for American Progress; conducted by Data for Progress.

source_title: In general, do you think now is a good time or a bad time to buy a house? / Do you think you will buy a home in the next five years?
question: In general, do you think now is a good time or a bad time to buy a house? / Do you think you will buy a home in the next five years?
method: Telephone (RDD, 80% cell/20% landline), conducted by ReconMR; weighted by gender, age, race, Hispanic ethnicity, education, party, region, population density
population: U.S. adults 18+, all 50 states and D.C.
editor_notes: Only 26% say it is a good time to buy a house (72% say bad time). A new low of 30% of non-homeowners plan to buy in the next 5 years. 68% of renters say they cannot afford to buy. This is Gallup's Economy and Personal Finance survey; 'support' interpreted as 'good time to buy.'

source_title: How would you rate your overall opinion of Medicare, the federal government health insurance program for adults 65 and older and for some younger adults with disabilities? [Favorable / Unfavorable] /
question: How would you rate your overall opinion of Medicare, the federal government health insurance program for adults 65 and older and for some younger adults with disabilities? [Favorable / Unfavorable] / Do you think the federal government spends too much, too little, or about the right amount on Medicare?
method: Online and telephone (SSRS Opinion Panel + RDD cell supplement); probability-based, Address-Based Sample recruitment; weighted to 2024 CPS
population: U.S. adults 18+, nationally representative (English and Spanish)
editor_notes: 82% hold a favorable view of Medicare (75% Republicans, 84% independents, 90% Democrats). 51% say federal government spends 'not enough' on Medicare; only 15% say 'too much.' 81% are 'very/somewhat worried' Medicare enrollees won't receive same benefit levels in future.

source_title: Is it the federal government's responsibility to make sure all Americans have health care coverage? [Yes / No]
question: Is it the federal government's responsibility to make sure all Americans have health care coverage? [Yes / No]
method: Pew American Trends Panel (ATP); online and telephone; address-based sample recruitment; SSRS conducted for Pew; weighted to U.S. adult population
editor_notes: 66% say yes (90% Democrats/Dem-leaners, 41% Republicans/GOP-leaners). Of those who say yes: 35% favor a single national government program, 31% prefer mix of public/private. 7% say government should not provide health insurance at all.

source_title: Do you support or oppose a Medicare for All system — described as a 'national health insurance program that would cover all Americans and replace most private health insurance plans'?
question: Do you support or oppose a Medicare for All system — described as a 'national health insurance program that would cover all Americans and replace most private health insurance plans'?
method: Web panel; weighted by age, gender, education, race, geography, recalled presidential vote
editor_notes: 65% initial support (78% Democrats, 71% independents, 49% Republicans). After being told it would 'eliminate most private insurance plans and replace premiums with higher taxes': 63% still support. After hearing balanced attack/defense arguments: 58% support. Oppose percentages not explicitly reported.

source_title: Do you think it is the responsibility of the federal government to make sure all Americans have healthcare coverage, or is that not the responsibility of the federal government? / Do you think the U.S
question: Do you think it is the responsibility of the federal government to make sure all Americans have healthcare coverage, or is that not the responsibility of the federal government? / Do you think the U.S. should have a government-run healthcare system, or a system based mostly on private health insurance?
method: Telephone (RDD, 80% cell/20% landline), conducted by ReconMR; random sample national adults
population: U.S. adults 18+, all 50 states and D.C.
editor_notes: 62% say it is the federal government's responsibility to ensure all Americans have healthcare coverage — the highest in more than a decade (was as low as 42% in 2013). 90% of Democrats, 65% of independents, 32% of Republicans agree. On system type: 46% prefer government-run; 49% prefer private insurance-based (closest divide since 2017). ACA approval at 54%.

source_title: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour for all workers? [Broad economic policy poll also including healthcare cost and housing affordability items]
question: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour for all workers? [Broad economic policy poll also including healthcare cost and housing affordability items]
editor_notes: NOTE: This Morning Consult poll (March 2026, n=1,100) covered economic policies broadly including housing and wages; no specific Medicare-for-All or universal healthcare question was identified in public summaries. Morning Consult tracks healthcare favorability but the March 2026 economic policy poll does not appear to include a Medicare expansion item. GAP: No Morning Consult poll specifically on universal healthcare/Medicare expansion as a standalone item was found from 2023–2026 in publicly accessible results.

source_title: In the Middle East situation, are your sympathies more with the Israelis or more with the Palestinians? / Do you favor or oppose the establishment of an independent Palestinian state alongside Israel?
question: In the Middle East situation, are your sympathies more with the Israelis or more with the Palestinians? / Do you favor or oppose the establishment of an independent Palestinian state alongside Israel?
method: Telephone (RDD, 80% cell/20% landline), conducted by ReconMR; ±4 pp margin of error
population: U.S. adults 18+, all 50 states and D.C.
editor_notes: 'Support' = % sympathizing more with Palestinians; 'Oppose' = % sympathizing more with Israelis; undecided = neither/no opinion. First time since 2001 Americans don't more with Israelis (41% Palestinians vs. 36% Israelis). 57% favor an independent Palestinian state (near 23-year high). 46% view Israel favorably vs. 37% Palestinian Territories.

source_title: Is Israel going too far, taking about the right approach, or not going far enough in its military operation against Hamas? / Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Israeli government?
question: Is Israel going too far, taking about the right approach, or not going far enough in its military operation against Hamas? / Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Israeli government?
method: Pew American Trends Panel (ATP); online and telephone (SSRS); address-based sample recruitment; weighted; ±1.9 pp margin of error
editor_notes: 'Support' = % saying Israel is going too far in military operation; 'Oppose' = % saying right approach (16%) + not going far enough (10%) combined = 26%. 59% unfavorable view of Israeli government (up from 51% in early 2024). 56% favorable view of Israeli people, 52% favorable view of Palestinian people. 42% disapprove of Trump administration's handling of the conflict; 30% approve.

source_title: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of Israel? [Country rating question] / Do you think a way can be found for Israel and an independent Palestinian state to coexist peacefully?
question: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of Israel? [Country rating question] / Do you think a way can be found for Israel and an independent Palestinian state to coexist peacefully?
method: Pew American Trends Panel (ATP); nationally representative; online and telephone; SSRS; weighted
editor_notes: 'Support' = favorable view of Israel; 'Oppose' = unfavorable. More than half (53%) now hold an unfavorable view of Israel — up from 42% in March 2022. 46% think a two-state solution can be found (down from 52% in late 2023). 32% have confidence in Netanyahu. Survey conducted around Netanyahu's U.S. visit.

source_title: Do you support or oppose the U.S. calling for a permanent ceasefire and de-escalation of violence in Gaza? / Do you think the U.S. should prioritize humanitarian aid for civilians in Gaza over militar
question: Do you support or oppose the U.S. calling for a permanent ceasefire and de-escalation of violence in Gaza? / Do you think the U.S. should prioritize humanitarian aid for civilians in Gaza over military aid and weapons for Israel?
method: Web panel; weighted by age, gender, education, race, geography, recalled presidential vote; ±3 pp margin of error
editor_notes: 76% support U.S. calling for permanent ceasefire in Gaza (record high; was 61% in Nov. 2023, 69% in June 2024). 54% say U.S. should prioritize humanitarian aid over military aid. 51% of voters oppose Israel's May 2025 military escalation into Gaza. 47% believe Israel is committing genocide in Gaza (up from 39% in April 2024).

source_title: Do you support or oppose Israel blocking the delivery of food and medical supplies into Gaza? / Do you think President Trump should demand that Israel let food and medical supplies into Gaza, or suppo
question: Do you support or oppose Israel blocking the delivery of food and medical supplies into Gaza? / Do you think President Trump should demand that Israel let food and medical supplies into Gaza, or support Israel in carrying out its military strategies against Hamas?
method: Web panel; weighted by age, gender, education, race, geography, recalled presidential vote; ±3 pp margin of error
editor_notes: 71% oppose Israel blocking delivery of food and medical supplies into Gaza (81% Democrats, 69% independents, 62% Republicans). 64% say Trump should demand Israel let aid into Gaza; 26% say he should support Israel's military strategies. 53% agree Israel has committed human rights abuses against Palestinians. Support/oppose here reflects opposition to the blockade.

source_title: When it comes to the conflict between Israelis and Palestinians, who do you sympathize with more — Israelis, Palestinians, or both sides equally? / Do you support or oppose the U.S. providing military
question: When it comes to the conflict between Israelis and Palestinians, who do you sympathize with more — Israelis, Palestinians, or both sides equally? / Do you support or oppose the U.S. providing military aid to Israel?
method: Morning Consult ongoing tracking survey (web panel)
editor_notes: 25% of voters sympathize most with Israelis (record low in 13 surveys since Oct. 2023). Only a net +7-point margin favoring Israelis over Palestinians. For the first time, voters are slightly more likely to oppose than support U.S. military aid to Israel (net 3-point opposition). Democrats and independents nearly 20 points more likely to oppose military aid. 43% of Republicans sympathize most with Israelis (down from 55% at war's outset). Exact oppose% for sympathy and military aid not stated in public release.

source_title: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour for all workers?
question: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour for all workers?
method: Morning Consult survey; ±3 pp margin of error
editor_notes: 75% of U.S. adults support raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour for all workers. Support holds across party lines (75% Republicans, 73% independents). Consistent across generations (75% Gen Z to 78% Gen X). Support is the same regardless of whether tips are excluded. Oppose% not published in public summary. Part of a broader 25-policy survey.

source_title: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $17 per hour? [Split sample: groups also asked about $9 and $12]
question: Do you support or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $17 per hour? [Split sample: groups also asked about $9 and $12]
method: Web panel; split-sample design; weighted to be representative of likely voters
editor_notes: 64% of voters support raising the minimum wage to $17/hour (85% Democrats, 65% independents, 45% Republicans). Support for $9/hr and $12/hr both exceed 80% across all parties. More than 80% of Democrats, independents, and Republicans say the current $7.25 federal minimum wage is not enough for a decent quality of life. Oppose% derived (100% - 64% support - assume ~0% undecided for this item).

source_title: Do you favor or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15 an hour? [Favor strongly / Favor somewhat / Oppose somewhat / Oppose strongly]
question: Do you favor or oppose raising the federal minimum wage to $15 an hour? [Favor strongly / Favor somewhat / Oppose somewhat / Oppose strongly]
method: Pew Research Center survey of 5,109 U.S. adults (mode not specified; likely ATP online/phone)
editor_notes: 62% favor raising the federal minimum wage to $15/hour (40% strongly favor). 38% oppose. Among Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents: 87% favor (61% strongly). Among Republicans and Republican-leaning independents: 72% oppose (45% strongly oppose). This is the most recent Pew poll specifically on the $15 federal minimum wage; no more recent Pew poll on this specific question was located.

source_title: Would you vote for or against a law that would raise the federal minimum wage to $9 an hour? / Would you vote for or against a law that would raise the federal minimum wage to $9 an hour with automati
question: Would you vote for or against a law that would raise the federal minimum wage to $9 an hour? / Would you vote for or against a law that would raise the federal minimum wage to $9 an hour with automatic increases tied to the inflation rate?
method: Gallup Daily telephone tracking survey (RDD)
population: U.S. adults 18+, all 50 states and D.C.
editor_notes: 76% say they would vote for raising the minimum wage to $9/hour (22% against). 69% would vote for $9 with automatic inflation increases (28% against). Support for $9 flat: 91% Democrats, 76% independents, 58% Republicans. Support for $9 + inflation: 74% Democrats, 69% independents, 43% Republicans. NOTE: This is Gallup's most recently available public poll specifically testing a minimum wage increase. Gallup has not published a publicly accessible minimum wage-specific poll with comparable data since 2013; their 2026 economic policy findings cite $15/hour support from Morning Consult methodology.

source_title: Polling record
Do you support or oppose the federal government canceling some or all student debt for all borrowers? / Do you think President Biden should continue fighting to cancel student loan debt?
question: Do you support or oppose the federal government canceling some or all student debt for all borrowers? / Do you think President Biden should continue fighting to cancel student loan debt?
method: Web panel; weighted to be representative of likely voters (SBPC/DfP tracking series)
editor_notes: 63% of likely voters support federal government action to cancel some or all student debt. Supported by 61% without a college degree, 58% without student debt, and 57% of voters over 45. Fewer than 1-in-3 (30%) say the federal government should not cancel any debt. Separately, a September 2024 DfP poll found a majority say Biden should 'continue fighting to cancel student loan debt' (69% of voters under 45).

source_title: Do you support or oppose the following: eliminating income-driven repayment options for student loan borrowers? / Do you support or oppose efforts to eliminate Parent PLUS and Graduate PLUS loans?
question: Do you support or oppose the following: eliminating income-driven repayment options for student loan borrowers? / Do you support or oppose efforts to eliminate Parent PLUS and Graduate PLUS loans?
method: Web panel; weighted to be representative of likely voters
editor_notes: 60% of voters (including a majority of Republicans) oppose eliminating income-driven repayment options. 63% believe federal funding should ensure access to affordable higher education. Majority oppose cuts to PSLF, oppose taxing scholarship/fellowship assistance, and oppose eliminating Parent/Graduate PLUS loans. Commissioned by Student Borrower Protection Center and Groundwork Collaborative; conducted by Data for Progress.

source_title: Do you support or oppose canceling up to $10,000 in student loan debt for each borrower?
question: Do you support or oppose canceling up to $10,000 in student loan debt for each borrower?
method: Online survey; unweighted margin of error ±2 pp; Morning Consult/Politico
editor_notes: 53% of registered voters support cancellation of up to $10,000 in student loan debt per borrower; 35% oppose. 79% support allowing student loan borrowers to defer payments without interest for 6 months. 85% Democrats support deferral; 75% Republicans support deferral. Less than 1-in-5 (21%) support government offering no student debt relief; 64% would oppose that approach. Conducted with Politico.

source_title: Student loan debt and financial security: share of adults with student loan debt who are not living comfortably / share who say their education was not worth the cost
question: Student loan debt and financial security: share of adults with student loan debt who are not living comfortably / share who say their education was not worth the cost
method: Pew Research Center analysis of Federal Reserve 2023 Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking
population: U.S. adults with student loan debt
editor_notes: Pew's 2024 student loan analysis focuses on descriptive data rather than a policy support/oppose question. Key findings: 25% of young college graduates with loans say they are 'finding it difficult to get by' or 'just getting by' financially (vs. 9% without loans). 35% say their degree was not worth the lifetime financial costs (vs. 16% without loans). This is not a cancellation opinion poll.

source_title: Student loan borrowers delaying major life events due to debt [descriptive tracking, not a cancellation policy question]
question: Student loan borrowers delaying major life events due to debt [descriptive tracking, not a cancellation policy question]
method: Web survey; current and prospective college students ages 18–59; Lumina Foundation/Gallup partnership
population: Current and prospective college students ages 18–59
editor_notes: Lumina Foundation–Gallup 2024 State of Higher Education study found 71% of borrowers delayed at least one major life event due to student loans. 63% of borrowers with <$10,000 in debt delayed milestones; 98% with debts over $60,000 delayed milestones. This is an impact/burden study, not a cancellation opinion poll. Survey fielded October–November 2023, published April 2024.
